coredata.dc:title: Construction of 0D/3D carbon quantum dots modified PbBiO2Cl microspheres with accelerated charge carriers for promoted visible-light-driven degradation of organic contaminants
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Wenqian
Hu, Qingsong
Wu, Ting
Wang, Zhixin
Yi, Jianjian
Yin, Sheng
coredata.dc:description: Photocatalysis is deemed as an effective technology to treat industrial wastewater. Whereas, the photocatalytic efficiency of conventional semiconductors is subjected to the poor absorption efficiency of visible-light photon and fast recombination rate of photoexcited electron-hole pairs. Hence, novel carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/PbBiO2Cl nanocomposite have been obtained via reactive ionic liquid, which acts as the template and reactant to induce the formation of PbBiO2Cl microspheres. When the loading content of CQDs is 0.5 wt%, the specific surface area of the nanocomposite is about 45.10 m2/g, much higher than single PbBiO2Cl (24.93 m2/g). Under the irradiation of visible light for 100 min and 105 min, the removal efficiency of rhodamine B and ciprofloxacin can reach 98% and 97% over 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl. And the corresponding reaction rate constant of 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl is 0.0335 min−1 and 0.0330 min−1, much higher than single PbBiO2Cl (0.0118 min−1 and 0.0156 min−1). The optical and electrochemical analysis reveal that the photocurrent response value of 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl is about 2 times higher than that of PbBiO2Cl. Additionally, the intensity of photoluminescence is lower than that of PbBiO2Cl. Consequently, the introduction of CQDs can effectively promote the absorbance of visible light, improve the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers, and the nanocomposite display enhanced photocatalytic activity. The strategy for the preparation of high-performance nanocomposite photocatalyst can be extended to develop more CQDs-based photocatalytic materials in the field of environment purification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
PbBiO2Cl microspheres
Ionic liquid
Nanocomposite photocatalyst
Visible light irradiation

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structure, morphology, composition and textural properties

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns, (b) FT-IR spectra of the obtained samples.
XPS spectra of PbBiO2Cl and 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl: (a) survey, (b) Pb 4 f, (c) Bi 4 f, (d) Cl 2p, (e) O 1 s, (f) C 1 s.
TEM and HRTEM images of 5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl.
Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and the corresponding pore-size distribution curves (inset) of (a) PbBiO2Cl and (b) 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl.

(a) Dynamic curves of RhB photodegradation under visible light irradiation, (b) plots of -In(C/C0) versus irradiation time, (c) photocatalytic degradation of CIP in the presence of PbBiO2Cl and 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl under visible light irradiation, (d) plots of -In(C/C0) versus irradiation time.
(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the obtained materials, (b) PL spectra, (c) transient photocurrent response and (d) EIS Nyquist plots of PbBiO2Cl and 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl.
Comparsion of the photocatalytic performance of 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl for the degradation of RhB with adding various radical scavengers under visible light irradiation.

DMPO spin-trapping ESR spectra recorded with PbBiO2Cl and 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl in (a,c) methanol dispersion (for DMPO-O2 •-) and (b,d) aqueous dispersion (for DMPO-•OH) under visible light irradiation.
Schematic diagram of the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers in the CQDs/PbBiO2Cl system combined with the possible photocatalytic mechanism.
Schematic diagram for the formation of CQDs modified PbBiO2Cl microspheres.

head.ce:title: Construction of 0D/3D carbon quantum dots modified PbBiO2Cl microspheres with accelerated charge carriers for promoted visible-light-driven degradation of organic contaminants

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University, 196 West Huayang Road, Yangzhou 225127, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University, 196 West Huayang Road, Yangzhou, 225127, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, 301 Xuefu Road, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, 301 Xuefu Road, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalysis is deemed as an effective technology to treat industrial wastewater. Whereas, the photocatalytic efficiency of conventional semiconductors is subjected to the poor absorption efficiency of visible-light photon and fast recombination rate of photoexcited electron-hole pairs. Hence, novel carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/PbBiO2Cl nanocomposite have been obtained via reactive ionic liquid, which acts as the template and reactant to induce the formation of PbBiO2Cl microspheres. When the loading content of CQDs is 0.5 wt%, the specific surface area of the nanocomposite is about 45.10 m2/g, much higher than single PbBiO2Cl (24.93 m2/g). Under the irradiation of visible light for 100 min and 105 min, the removal efficiency of rhodamine B and ciprofloxacin can reach 98% and 97% over 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl. And the corresponding reaction rate constant of 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl is 0.0335 min−1 and 0.0330 min−1, much higher than single PbBiO2Cl (0.0118 min−1 and 0.0156 min−1). The optical and electrochemical analysis reveal that the photocurrent response value of 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl is about 2 times higher than that of PbBiO2Cl. Additionally, the intensity of photoluminescence is lower than that of PbBiO2Cl. Consequently, the introduction of CQDs can effectively promote the absorbance of visible light, improve the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers, and the nanocomposite display enhanced photocatalytic activity. The strategy for the preparation of high-performance nanocomposite photocatalyst can be extended to develop more CQDs-based photocatalytic materials in the field of environment purification.

ce:section.ce:para: The increasingly serious water pollution caused by industrialization and urbanization has attracted a lot of attention. In particular, a large amount of refractory contaminants pose a serious threat to aquatic organisms and public health [1]. It is urgent to remove the residual aromatic organics in the environment. Among various treatment techniques, the development of novel processes to generate reactive radicals is highly appreciated in environment renovation [2]. Semiconductor photocatalysis is regarded as one of the most efficient technologies for radicals generation owing to its environmental friendliness and low energy consumption [3,4]. Nevertheless, traditional photocatalysts display two major shortcomings, First, traditional semiconductor photocatalyst (TiO2, ZnO) only absorbs UV light owing to the wide band gap, while UV light occupies almost 4% of the entire solar spectrum. Second, the photocatalytic efficiency is restricted to the high recombination rate of electron-hole pairs [5,6]. For this purpose, various strategies have been conducted to overcome these obstacles, such as, morphology control [7], elemental doping [8], defect engineering [9], heterojunction [10], dye sensitization [11], and so on. Among which, the heterojunction can effectively regulate the performance of single component, and generate many new properties. Therefore, rational construction of heterojunction can break through the mentioned bottlenecks, thus high-performance photocatalyst can be obtained.

Over the past decade, bismuth oxyhalides, BiOX (X = Cl, Br and I), a novel class of layered material, has attracted extensive attention in the field of environment purification and energy conversion [12]. They display preferable photocatalytic activity in the area of contaminants degradation, nitrogen fixation, oxygen evolution, CO2 reduction, and so on [13–15]. Whereas, the further development of BiOX is limited to their intrinsic shortcomings, for example, low visible-light absorption capacity for BiOCl and BiOBr, high recombination efficiency of photogenerated carriers. Hence, a sequence of strategies have been conducted to enhance the photocatalytic activity of BiOX, such as bismuth-rich strategy, elemental doping, defect regulation, interface engineering [16–18]. On account of the relevant references, a portion of Bi in the [Bi2O2]2+ layer is substituted by other metal elements (Ca, Sr, Ba, Pb, etc) form a covalent bimetallic oxide ion ([ABiO2]+) [19,20]. The [X]- and [ABiO2]+ ions are arranged alternately to construct ABiO2X samples. The formation of bismuth-based bimetallic oxyhalides can optimize the photocatalytic activity of bismuth oxyhalide under visible light irradiation. In consideration of the similar radius of Pb2+ and Bi3+, compared to pure BiOX, the crystal structure of BiOX do not change significantly via replacing Bi3+ with Pb2+ [21,22]. Among which, the novel layered material PbBiO2Cl has caused wide public concern in the area of environment purification and energy conversion, owing to its high stability, controllable morphology, and suitable band gap [23,24]. Even so, the photocatalytic performance of PbBiO2Cl can be further enhanced to meet the needs of industrial application.

In this decade, a novel carbon-based nanomaterial, 0D carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with sizes below 10 nm have been studied extensively [25,26]. The unique properties, such as, inexpensive, high chemical stability, low toxicity make them applicable in various fields [27]. Additionally, the conjugated π structure of CQDs endows them with excellent electron transfer ability [28]. Therefore, CQDs can be introduced to modify photocatalysts, which can broaden the visible-light absorption range, promote the effective separation and transporation of photogenerated carriers, hence improve their photocatalytic activity [29,30]. Even so, in most reported photocatalytic system, the key role of CQDs in the photocatalytic system has not been investigated in detail. Furthermore, how to uniformly anchor CQDs onto the surface of catalyst is also the key factor in improving efficient utilization of CQDs, thus should be further studied.

In order to overcome the above challenging issues, CQDs modified PbBiO2Cl nanocomposite have been obtained via self-sacrificing ionic liquid glue, which benefits for anchoring more CQDs on the surface of PbBiO2Cl microspheres, due to the existence of hydrogen bond and coulomb force between CQDs and ionic liquid [31]. The successful construction of CQDs/PbBiO2Cl heterojunction with intimate contact accelerate the migration of photogenerated carriers across heterojunction interface owing to the intense physical and electronic coupling effects. The incorporation of CQDs can also broaden the light absorption range, thus displaying enhanced photocatalytic performance towards the removal of rhodamine B (RhB) and ciprofloxacin (CIP) under visible light irradiation. This work provides new insights for the design and synthesis of more CQDs modified photocatalysts with broad applications in the field of environmental protection.

All the chemicals are of analytical grade and employed without further purification. CQDs was prepared on account of the previous reported literature [26]. 1.05 g citric acid was firstly dissolved into distilled water (10 mL), then ethylenediamine (335 μL) was added and magnetic mixed for 1 h. This solution was transfered into Teflon-lined autoclave (25 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After that, the reddish-brown solution was transferred into the dialysis bag and dialyzed for 24 h. Finally, CQDs powder was obtained after freeze drying.

The preparation process of single PbBiO2Cl and CQDs/PbBiO2Cl nanocomposite was as follows: Firstly, 0.48 g Bi(NO3)3.5 H2O and 0.33 g Pb(NO3)2 were added into 20 mL ethylene glycol and stirred for 0.5 h. Then, 0.34 g ionic liquid [C16mim]Cl was added to form a homogeneous system. After that, a certain amount of CQDs was added and stirred for 0.5 h. The evenly dispersed solution was transferred into 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 24 h. After reaction finished, the precipitate was collected by centrifugation, washed five times with ethanol and deionized water, and dried at 70 °C for 24 h. The added amount of CQDs in CQDs/PbBiO2Cl nanocomposite was 0.3, 0.5, and 1 wt%, respectively. Single PbBiO2Cl was synthesized without adding CQDs.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were acquired on a XRD-600X-ray diffractometer (Shimadzu, Japan) employing Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm). The morphologies of the catalysts were studied by scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-7001 F, JEOL, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin, FEI, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on an ESCALAB 250 (Thermo Scientific) employing Mg-Kα source. 2460 V2.01 apparatus (Micromeritics Instrument Corporation, USA) was used to obtain the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) value and pore size distributions. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) was tested on a UV-3600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) with a wavelength range of 200–1000 nm. FT-IR spectra was carried out on a Nexus 470 spectrophotometer (Thermo, USA) with a spectral resolution of 4 cm−1. PL spectra was collected on a Varian Cary Eclipse spectrometer. A JES-FA200 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) was used to collect electron spin resonance (ESR) signals by radical trapping reagent DMPO in water and methanol solution. A Xenon lamp source (PLS-SXE300D/300DUV, Beijing Perfectlight) was employed during the photocurrent test. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out in a 0.1 M KCl solution, which consists of 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3-/Fe(CN)6 4-. The electrochemical test was conducted on the CHI 760E electrochemical system.

RhB and CIP were specified as the target pollutants. A 300 W Xenon lamp acted as light source which only allow visible light (λ＞400 nm) available. Air was pumped into the reaction system and magnetic stirring was kept during the whole process. The photocatalytic degradation experiment was as follows: 0.03 g sample was dispersed in RhB or CIP solution (100 mL, 10 mg/L) with ultrasonic treatment for 10 min. The suspension was stirred in darkness for 0.5 h to obtain adsorption-desorption equilibrium. After that, Xenon lamp was turned on, and 4 mL reaction solution was withdrawn at set interval. The reaction solution was centrifuged at 13000 rpm for 5 min to remove the solid catalyst. The concentrations of RhB and CIP were analyzed by a UV–vis spectrometer at the wavelength of 553 nm and 276 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure, morphology, composition and textural properties
ce:section.ce:para: The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of PbBiO2Cl and CQDs/PbBiO2Cl nanocomposite with different CQDs contents are shown in Fig. 1a. The diffraction peaks of the obtained samples at 2θ = 14.3°, 23.6°, 31.2°, 32.2°, 43.7°, 46.0°, 48.3°, 52.3°, 55.3°, 56.8°, 65.3°, 67.1° and 76.0° correspond to crystal faces of (002), (111), (113), (020), (006), (220), (222), (131), (026), (117), (226), (040) and (137) on account of the standard card (JCPDS No. 13–0352). In addition, the diffraction peaks of CQDs/PbBiO2Cl nanocomposite do not shift significantly in comparison to pure PbBiO2Cl, indicating that the introduction of CQDs pose no effect on the crystal structure of PbBiO2Cl. The diffraction peaks of CQDs can not be observed, which may be attributable to the low doping amount and good dispersion of CQDs [32]. Simultaneously, no other peaks can be detected in the XRD patterns of CQDs/PbBiO2Cl nanocomposite, confirming the high purity of the catalysts. The successful preparation of CQDs/PbBiO2Cl nanocomposite will be further proved by other characterizations.( Scheme 1).

The chemical spectra and molecular structure of the obtained samples were analyzed by FT-IR spectra. As depicted in Fig. 1b, it can be found that the nanocomposite display characteristic peaks centered at 523, 1430, 1494 and 1663 cm−1. And the strong characteristic peak at 523 cm−1 can be attributable to the Bi-O stretching vibration [33]. The peak at 1430 cm−1 can be attributable to the symmetric carboxylate stretch, while the peak at 1494 cm−1 can be assigned to the bending vibration of N-H [34]. Additionally, the peak at 1663 cm−1 is arisen from the CO stretching [26]. Nevertheless, these characteristic peaks shift to 1434, 1480 and 1651 cm−1 for pure CQDs. This can be attributable to the successful interaction between CQDs and PbBiO2Cl [26]. The FT-IR results indicate that CQDs have been successfully introduced into PbBiO2Cl microspheres during solvothermal synthesis.

The chemical element composition and chemical state of PbBiO2Cl and 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl were further analyzed by XPS spectroscopy. Fig. 2a displays a full spectrum of the as-prepared samples, which indicates that the main constituent elements of PbBiO2Cl and 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl are Pb, Bi, O, Cl, and C, respectively. For the sake of determining the valence state of each element in the samples, the high-resolution XPS analysis are shown in Fig. 2b-f. Fig. 2b shows that the two characteristic peaks of Pb 4 f are located at 138.4 eV and 143.2 eV, which are consistent with the Pb2+ binding energy in PbBiO2Cl material. However, the characteristic peaks of Pb 4 f in 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl shifts to 138.5 eV and 143.3 eV, which may be attributed to the interaction between PbBiO2Cl and CQDs [23]. Fig. 2c displays a high-resolution spectrum of Bi 4 f, the two strong peaks with binding energies of 159.4 and 164.7 eV correspond to Bi 4 f 7/2 and Bi 4 f 5/2 orbitals, respectively. Fig. 2d displays the Cl 2p peak, which is consistent with the presence of Cl− centered at the binding energies of 198.2 and 199.8 eV. The peak of O 1 s at 530.1 eV can be owing to the oxygen element in PbBiO2Cl (Fig. 2e). Fig. 2f shows a high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s with signals located at 284.4, 285.3 and 287.4 eV, corresponding to the C-C, CC and C-N, respectively [34]. The results of XPS analysis are consistent with the FT-IR spectra, which further confirm the coexistence of CQDs and PbBiO2Cl in the nanocomposite.

To further investigate the microstructures of CQDs/PbBiO2Cl nanocomposite, SEM and TEM characterizations were carried out. In Fig. S1, 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl displays a typical microspheric structure, which consists of abundant nanosheets. Given the plentiful functional groups on the surface of CQDs, they can serve as the nucleation sites for the self-assembly of nanosheets [34]. In Fig. 3a-c, be found that the diameter of monodispersed sphere is about 1 µm. And a great deal of CQDs are dispersed around the surface of the microspheres. In addition, high-resolution TEM characterization was employed to explore the distribution of CQDs in the nanocomposite. As depicted in Fig. 3d, the lattice spacing is measured to be 0.28 and 0.33 nm, which correspond to (020) crystal plane of PbBiO2Cl and (002) crystal plane of CQDs [23,26]. The above characterization analysis indicate that CQDs are uniformly distributed on the surface of PbBiO2Cl microspheres. Simultaneously, the elemental mapping images of 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl are displayed in Fig. S2. It can be found that the C, Pb, Bi, O and Cl elements are evenly distributed, further confirming the existence of CQDs in the nanocomposite.

Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms were conducted to obtain the specific surface area and pore size distribution of the obtained samples. As depicted in Fig. 4, the BET value of single PbBiO2Cl is calculated to be 24.93 m2/g. After the introduction of CQDs, the BET value of 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl is 45.10 m2/g, which is about 2 times higher than that of PbBiO2Cl. It is well established that the increased specific surface area can provide great contact surface between catalyst and organic contaminants [35]. To further confirm this assumption, the adsorption experiments are carried out. As depicted in Fig. S3, all the samples display some adsorption performance towards RhB and CIP. And it is interesting to find that the adsorption capacity increases slightly with the loading amount of CQDs. This is in accord with the results of specific surface area.

The photocatalytic activity of PbBiO2Cl and CQDs/PbBiO2Cl nanocomposite was assessed via the degradation of RhB and CIP under visible light irradiation. As depicted in Fig. 5a, CQDs/PbBiO2Cl nanocomposite display significantly enhanced photocatalytic activity towards the removal of RhB. Among which, 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl displays the highest degradation rate. Nevertheless, when the loading amount of CQDs is over 0.5 wt%, the degradation rate decreases. This may be explained by the fact that too many CQDs can prevent PbBiO2Cl surface from absorbing visible-light photons, resulting in weak light exposure of PbBiO2Cl for the production of photo-induced carriers [32]. In the meantime, the degradation kinetics of RhB by the catalysts are explored. In Fig. 5b, the concentration variation of RhB versus the reaction time abide by the pseudo-first-order kinetic plot. 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl shows the highest degradation rate, about 2.8 times higher than that of PbBiO2Cl. Furthermore, CIP was selected as the model pollutant in order to eliminate the dye sensitization in the degradation process. Fig. 5c displays the degradation plots of CIP over PbBiO2Cl and 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl. After illumination for 105 min, about 98% of CIP can be degraded over 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl, while the removal rate of single PbBiO2Cl is only 79%. The reaction rate constant of 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl is about 2.1 times higher than that of PbBiO2Cl (Fig. 5d). To further confirm the photostability of the catalyst, the recycling experiments are carried out. As depicted in Fig. S4, about 90% of RhB and CIP can be degraded over 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl after five sequential cycles. This can be explained that the as-prepared catalysts remain stable during the photodegradation process.

It is well known that the spectral response property mainly affect photocatalytic activity of semiconductor. Fig. 6a displays the light absorption range of the obtained samples. It can be observed that the absorption edge of PbBiO2Cl is about 490 nm. Pure CQDs displays preferable absorption property in the visible-light region. Therefore, the introduction of CQDs can significantly improve the absorption of visible light for PbBiO2Cl. Photoluminescence (PL) technique has been conducted to explore the generation, transportation and separation efficiency of photo-induced carriers during the photocatalytic process. It is generally accepted that the weaker PL spectrum represents a lower recombination rate of photo-induced carriers [36,37]. In Fig. 6b, both PbBiO2Cl and 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl display strong emission peak located at 470 nm. For comparsion, the intensity of emission peak for 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl significantly weakens, indicating that the introduction of CQDs can effectively inhibit the recombination of photo-induced carriers.

Given that the optical test results, it can be speculated that the incorporation of CQDs can effectively lower the recombination rate of photo-induced carriers, which is helpful in improving the photocatalytic efficiency. In order to prove this assumption, electrochemical analysis were carried out. Fig. 6c shows the photocurrent-time (I-t) curves of PbBiO2Cl and 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl electrodes. After four inconsecutive on-off photoirradiation cycles, the photocurrent values keep stable. And the photocurrent value of 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl is about 2 times higher than that of PbBiO2Cl. This indicates that CQDs/PbBiO2Cl nanocomposite display higher separation and migration rate of photo-induced carriers. EIS analysis of PbBiO2Cl and 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl are displayed in Fig. 6d. 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl shows a much smaller arc radius than that of PbBiO2Cl. This demonstrates that the fast transportation of photo-induced charge to electron acceptor arises and contributes to effective separation of photoexcited electron-hole pairs [38]. The analysis of transient photocurrent and EIS are in accordance with PL measurement, which indicates that the anchoring of CQDs is a promising strategy to improve catalytic efficiency.

It is commonly known that the degradation of organic pollutants involves the exploit of various active radicals, such as superoxide radical (O2 •-), hydroxyl radical (·OH), and so on. To determine the major active species involved into the photocatalytic system of CQDs/PbBiO2Cl nanocomposite, radical quenching experiment was carried out via adding various free-radical scavengers (IPA for ·OH, TEOA for h+, N2 for O2 •-). As depicted in Fig. 7, the photocatalytic efficiency remains stable after the addition of IPA, indicating that ·OH is not the major reactive species. Nevertheless, the photocatalytic efficiency decreases obviously after the addition of TEOA or N2, indicating that h+ and O2 •- are the major active species involved in the photocatalytic reaction. To further confirm this assumption, ESR technique was conducted to detect reactive oxygen species. As shown in Fig. 8a and c, the characteristic signal peaks of DMPO-·O2 - can be detected over PbBiO2Cl and 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl under illumination. And the peak intensity of 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl is stronger than that of PbBiO2Cl. For O2 •- derives from the reduction of O2 through single electron transfer, the higher intensity of O2 •- demonstrates that CQDs enable more photoexcited electrons to participate in the reduction of O2, owing to its particular delocalized conjugated structure [26]. Nevertheless, the characteristic signal peaks of ·OH can not be detected over PbBiO2Cl and 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl under visible light irradiation (Fig. 8b and d). This implies that ·OH is not the major radical species involved into the photocatalytic reaction, which is in accord with the results of radical quenching experiment.

As we all know that, the band alignment plays as a crucial role in determining the photocatalytic efficiency. In Fig. S5, the positive slopes of Mott-Schotty plots demonstrate that PbBiO2Cl is a n-type semiconductor, and the flat band potential of PbBiO2Cl is about − 0.62 V vs. NHE (pH 7) on account of the extrapolation of X intercept in the Mott-Schotty plots [39]. For n-type semiconductor, the Fermi level is approximate to the flat band potential. Due to the valence-band (VB) XPS spectra with the Fermi level of semiconductors is 0 eV (Fig. S6), the VB value of PbBiO2Cl is 1.2 V vs. NHE. On account of the bandgap width obtained by DRS characterization (Fig. S7), the conduction band (CB) value can be calculated on the basis of E
CB = E
VB – E
g . The CB value is about − 1.28 V. Considering that the above experimental analysis, a feasible photocatalytic mechanism is proposed ( Fig. 9). Under illumination, the electrons can be excitated and migrate to the CB of PbBiO2Cl. Whereas, the photoexcited electrons can rapidly recombine with holes, which is unpopular during the photocatalytic reaction. After the introduction of CQDs, the photoexcited electrons can migrate from the CB of PbBiO2Cl to CQDs. Thanks to the particular delocalized conjugated structure of CQDs, the photoexcited electrons can be migrated quickly, which contributes to the improvement of photocatalytic activity. This is consistent with the analysis of PL, photocurrent and EIS measurements. Meanwhile, the electrons on CQDs can restore the adsorbed oxygen molecules to generate more O2 •-. Both O2 •- and holes are the major active species during the radical reactions.

In summary, a sequence of visible-light-responsive CQDs/PbBiO2Cl nanocomposite have been prepared via ionic liquid assisted solvothermal method. 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl displayed the optimizing photocatalytic performance towards the removal of RhB and CIP under photoirradiation. The chemical composition and microstructure of CQDs/PbBiO2Cl nanocomposite were studied via XRD, XPS, SEM, TEM, FT-IR and BET analysis. Additionally, optical and electrochemical properties (UV–vis DRS, PL, EIS and photocurrent response) as well as ESR measurements were adopted to analyze the improvement photocatalytic performance of CQDs/PbBiO2Cl nanocomposite. The promoted visible-light harvesting, high charge mobility and active sites can be obtained owing to the tight interface contact. Furthermore, the mechanism for the degradation of organic contaminants can be proposed. Our research extends the knowledge into developing novel visible-light-responsive nanocomposite catalyst in the field of environmental remediation.

Wenqian Sun: Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Qingsong Hu: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Ting Wu: Data curation, Investigation. Zhixin Wang: Data curation, Investigation. Jianjian Yi: Writing – review & editing. Sheng Yin: Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no competing financial interests that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21376109), the State Key Laboratory of Pollution Control and Resource Reuse Foundation (NO. PCRRF20019), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (NO. 2021M691389).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.128591.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in single-atom catalysts for advanced oxidation process
